beginner.summary: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard that allows AI models to connect to external tools, data sources, and services in a structured, secure way. Think of it as a USB standard for AI — any tool that speaks MCP can plug into any AI model that supports it.
key_concepts: MCP host: the application running the AI model (e.g. Claude Desktop)
MCP server: a lightweight process that exposes tools and resources
MCP client: the component inside the host that talks to servers
Tools: functions the AI can call (e.g. get_weather)
Resources: files or data the AI can read
Transport: how messages travel (stdio, HTTP/SSE)
common_misconceptions: MCP is not an AI model itself — it is a communication protocol
MCP servers do not have to be remote — they can run locally on your machine
MCP does not replace function calling — it standardizes how tools are discovered and invoked
beginner.next_step: Try running a simple FastMCP server with one tool and connect to it from a Python client.

intermediate.summary: MCP enables agentic systems to discover and call tools dynamically. At the intermediate level you understand the full request-response lifecycle, tool schemas, tag-based filtering, and how to build multi-tool MCP servers used inside LLM agentic loops.
key_concepts: Tool discovery: list_tools() returns JSON schemas for all registered tools
Tag filtering: tools can be tagged and clients can filter by tag
Agentic loop: LLM calls list_tools, receives tool_calls, executes via call_tool, feeds results back
FastMCP: Python framework that makes building MCP servers easy with decorators
Transport options: stdio for local processes, HTTP/SSE for remote servers
Error handling: structured error responses vs exceptions
common_misconceptions: LLMs do not automatically know which tools exist — they need list_tools output injected into context
Tag filtering happens client-side, not server-side — all tools are still registered on the server
FastMCP tools are sync or async — mixing is fine
intermediate.next_step: Build an agentic loop that uses at least 2 MCP tools in sequence and passes results between them.

advanced.summary: At the advanced level, MCP is used to build production-grade tool ecosystems. You design tool taxonomies, implement resource management, handle auth, optimize latency, and integrate MCP servers into multi-agent architectures like A2A.
key_concepts: Resource management: exposing structured data (files, DB records) as MCP resources
Prompts: reusable prompt templates registered as MCP prompts
Sampling: server-initiated LLM calls via MCP sampling messages
Auth: OAuth2 flows for securing MCP server endpoints
Multi-agent integration: MCP servers shared across multiple A2A agents
Observability: logging, tracing, and monitoring MCP tool calls in production
common_misconceptions: MCP resources are not the same as RAG — resources are structured references, not vector search results
Sampling does not mean the server controls the LLM — it sends a request the host can reject
Production MCP servers should be stateless where possible to support horizontal scaling
advanced.next_step: Design and implement a shared MCP server used by 3 or more A2A agents with different tool subsets per agent.

beginner.summary: A2A (Agent-to-Agent) is an open protocol developed by Google that lets AI agents communicate with each other regardless of which framework or model they use. It defines a standard way for one agent to send tasks to another and receive streaming results.
key_concepts: Agent Card: a JSON manifest that describes an agent's capabilities and URL
Task: a unit of work sent from one agent to another
Skill: a named capability an agent advertises in its Agent Card
Orchestrator: an agent that routes tasks to other (remote) agents
Remote agent: a specialist agent that receives tasks from an orchestrator
JSON-RPC: the transport format used for A2A messages
common_misconceptions: A2A is not a chatbot framework — it is a protocol for agent interoperability
Agents do not need to use the same LLM or framework to communicate via A2A
The Agent Card is public and unauthenticated by design — it describes capabilities, not secrets
beginner.next_step: Run a simple A2A agent that returns a greeting, and call it from a Python client using the A2A SDK.

intermediate.summary: At the intermediate level you build multi-agent systems using A2A. You understand how orchestrators discover and route to remote agents, how streaming works, how context_id enables multi-turn conversations, and how to integrate MCP tools inside A2A agents.
key_concepts: Agent discovery: orchestrators fetch Agent Cards from known URLs
Keyword routing: matching user input to agent skills via keyword overlap
Streaming: agents yield partial results via TaskArtifactUpdateEvent
context_id: shared ID that links turns in a multi-turn conversation
TaskUpdater: helper class for streaming task state changes
BaseAgentExecutor: shared executor pattern for all agents in a system
common_misconceptions: context_id is not automatically threaded through — you must forward it explicitly
Streaming does not mean real-time tokens — it means the agent can yield multiple events
Orchestrators do not merge LLM responses from multiple agents — they forward them separately
intermediate.next_step: Build an orchestrator that routes between 3 specialist agents using Agent Card keyword matching and supports multi-turn conversation.

advanced.summary: Advanced A2A covers production patterns: auth between agents, agent health checks, dynamic agent discovery, hierarchical orchestration, and integrating A2A with CI/CD pipelines. You also understand the A2A specification at the protocol level.
key_concepts: Push notifications: agents sending async task updates via webhooks
Agent authentication: securing agent-to-agent calls with tokens
Dynamic discovery: agents registering themselves in a service registry
Hierarchical orchestration: orchestrators calling other orchestrators
Task lifecycle: SUBMITTED → WORKING → COMPLETED/FAILED/INPUT_REQUIRED
A2A SDK internals: LegacyRequestHandler, InMemoryTaskStore, task routing
common_misconceptions: Push notifications are optional — most workshop systems use pull-based streaming
InMemoryTaskStore is not suitable for production — use a persistent store
The A2A spec is still evolving — always check the latest SDK version
advanced.next_step: Add agent authentication to a multi-agent system and implement a dynamic agent registry.

beginner.summary: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is a technique where an AI model is given relevant documents retrieved from a knowledge base before generating an answer. Instead of relying only on training data, the model can answer questions about private or up-to-date information.
key_concepts: Vector embedding: turning text into numerical vectors that capture meaning
Vector store: a database that stores and searches embeddings (e.g. FAISS, Pinecone)
Retrieval: finding the most relevant chunks of text for a given query
Augmentation: injecting retrieved text into the LLM prompt as context
Chunking: splitting long documents into smaller pieces before embedding
Similarity search: finding vectors closest to the query vector (cosine similarity)
common_misconceptions: RAG does not make the LLM remember documents permanently — it retrieves fresh each time
Larger chunks are not always better — too large reduces precision, too small loses context
RAG does not eliminate hallucinations — the LLM can still ignore or misinterpret retrieved context
beginner.next_step: Build a simple RAG pipeline: embed 5 documents, store in a vector DB, retrieve top-2 for a query, and feed to an LLM.

intermediate.summary: Intermediate RAG covers advanced retrieval strategies, reranking, hybrid search, and evaluation. You understand how to build production RAG pipelines with chunking strategies, metadata filtering, and quality metrics.
key_concepts: Hybrid search: combining vector search with keyword (BM25) search
Reranking: using a cross-encoder to reorder retrieved results by relevance
Metadata filtering: pre-filtering documents by date, category, or source
Parent-child chunking: small chunks for retrieval, large chunks for context
RAG evaluation: RAGAS metrics (faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision)
Query expansion: rewriting the user query to improve retrieval recall
common_misconceptions: Reranking is not always needed — it adds latency and is most useful for large retrieval sets
Hybrid search requires both a vector store and a keyword index — more infrastructure
High retrieval recall does not guarantee high generation quality
intermediate.next_step: Add a reranker to your RAG pipeline and measure the improvement using RAGAS faithfulness score.

advanced.summary: Advanced RAG covers agentic RAG (the LLM decides when and how to retrieve), knowledge graph RAG, multi-hop reasoning, and production optimization including caching, streaming, and cost control.
key_concepts: Agentic RAG: LLM decides whether to retrieve, what to retrieve, and when to stop
Knowledge graph RAG: combining vector search with graph traversal for structured reasoning
Multi-hop retrieval: chaining multiple retrieval steps to answer complex questions
Contextual compression: summarizing retrieved chunks before injecting into context
Semantic caching: caching LLM responses for similar queries to reduce cost
Streaming RAG: streaming retrieved context and LLM output simultaneously
common_misconceptions: Agentic RAG is more powerful but also more expensive and slower — use only when needed
Knowledge graphs require significant upfront investment — not always justified
Semantic caching invalidation is non-trivial — stale cache can cause incorrect answers
advanced.next_step: Implement agentic RAG where the agent can decide to retrieve 0, 1, or 2 times based on confidence.

beginner.summary: Prompt engineering is the practice of designing inputs to AI models to get reliable, high-quality outputs. A well-crafted prompt gives the model the right role, context, instructions, and format to follow.
key_concepts: System prompt: sets the AI's role, tone, and constraints
Zero-shot: asking the model to complete a task without examples
Few-shot: providing 2-5 examples of input-output pairs before the task
Chain-of-thought: asking the model to 'think step by step' before answering
Instruction clarity: being specific about format, length, and what NOT to do
Temperature: controls randomness (0 = deterministic, 1 = creative)
common_misconceptions: Longer prompts are not always better — unnecessary context can confuse the model
The model does not 'remember' previous conversations unless history is included
Chain-of-thought helps reasoning but increases token cost
beginner.next_step: Take one task you currently do with a simple prompt and rewrite it with a system prompt, 3 few-shot examples, and output format instructions.

intermediate.summary: Intermediate prompt engineering covers structured output, tool-use prompting, prompt chaining, and meta-prompting. You understand how to prompt for JSON output, build multi-step pipelines, and evaluate prompt quality systematically.
key_concepts: Structured output: prompting for valid JSON or XML with schema enforcement
Tool-use prompting: instructing the model when and how to call tools
Prompt chaining: feeding output of one prompt as input to the next
ReAct pattern: Reason + Act — model alternates between thinking and tool calls
Prompt versioning: tracking prompt changes like code (with evals)
Negative examples: showing the model what NOT to do in few-shot
common_misconceptions: Structured output does not guarantee valid JSON — always validate and retry
ReAct is not a framework — it is a prompting pattern any LLM can follow
Prompt chaining increases latency proportionally to the number of steps
intermediate.next_step: Build a 3-step prompt chain that extracts entities, classifies intent, and generates a structured response — all via prompting only.

advanced.summary: Advanced prompt engineering covers adversarial robustness, automatic prompt optimization, constitutional AI principles, and large-scale prompt management in production systems.
key_concepts: Prompt injection: attacks where user input overrides system instructions
Adversarial robustness: designing prompts that resist manipulation
APE (Automatic Prompt Engineer): using an LLM to generate and evaluate prompts
DSPy: a framework for programmatic prompt optimization with gradient-like feedback
Constitutional AI: embedding ethical rules directly in system prompts
Prompt registry: centralized versioned storage for production prompts
common_misconceptions: No prompt is fully injection-proof — defense-in-depth is required
APE/DSPy require a labeled evaluation set — they are not zero-setup
Constitutional AI does not replace safety training — it is an additional layer
advanced.next_step: Set up a prompt registry with version tracking and automated evals that run on every prompt change.

beginner.summary: Python async programming lets your code do multiple things at once without using multiple threads. Instead of blocking and waiting (e.g. for a web request), async code pauses at `await` points and lets other tasks run in the meantime.
key_concepts: async def: defines a coroutine function
await: pauses execution until the awaited coroutine completes
asyncio.run(): entry point that runs an async function from synchronous code
Event loop: the scheduler that runs coroutines and IO callbacks
asyncio.gather(): run multiple coroutines concurrently
Coroutine: a function that can be paused and resumed
common_misconceptions: async does not make your code faster by default — it is useful only for IO-bound tasks
You cannot await in regular (non-async) functions
asyncio is single-threaded — it does not use multiple CPU cores
beginner.next_step: Write an async function that fetches 5 URLs concurrently using asyncio.gather() and measures the time savings vs sequential.

intermediate.summary: Intermediate async covers tasks, queues, timeouts, async context managers, and async generators. You understand how to build concurrent pipelines, handle cancellation, and integrate async with frameworks like FastAPI or Starlette.
key_concepts: asyncio.create_task(): schedule a coroutine to run concurrently
asyncio.Queue: async-safe producer-consumer queue
asyncio.timeout() / asyncio.wait_for(): add timeouts to coroutines
Async context managers: __aenter__ / __aexit__ for resource management
Async generators: yield values from async functions (async for ...)
Task cancellation: task.cancel() + asyncio.CancelledError handling
common_misconceptions: create_task() and gather() both run concurrently but gather() waits for all — create_task() fires and forgets
Async context managers are not automatically thread-safe
CancelledError should usually be re-raised, not silently caught
intermediate.next_step: Implement an async pipeline with a producer that generates items, a queue, and 3 concurrent consumers that process items.

advanced.summary: Advanced async Python covers event loop internals, custom executors for CPU-bound work, async debugging, profiling, and patterns for building high-throughput async services.
key_concepts: loop.run_in_executor(): offload CPU-bound work to a thread/process pool
uvloop: faster event loop implementation (drop-in replacement for asyncio)
Structured concurrency: anyio / trio patterns for safer task management
AsyncExitStack: managing multiple async context managers dynamically
Debugging async code: asyncio debug mode, tracemalloc, async stack traces
Backpressure: controlling producer speed to avoid queue overflow
common_misconceptions: uvloop is not compatible with all platforms (Windows support is limited)
run_in_executor does not magically make CPU-bound code faster without a ProcessPoolExecutor
Structured concurrency is a design pattern, not a built-in Python feature
advanced.next_step: Profile an async service under load using asyncio debug mode and identify the top 3 bottlenecks.
